schools.summary: After the painful parting from the Ephesian elders (the wrenching separation, Robertson), Paul sailed by Coos, Rhodes, and Patara — a straight course down the coast, port by port; the precise eyewitness itinerary testifying to Luke's careful record.

themes: after we were gotten from them, and had launched

schools.summary: At Patara Paul found a ship sailing directly across to Phenicia (the coast of Syria) and embarked — a more direct route than coasting, hastening the journey toward Jerusalem for Pentecost; the providence in the timely ship.

themes: finding a ship sailing over unto Phenicia
we went aboard, and set forth

schools.summary: The ship came in sight of Cyprus, passed south of it, and sailed to Tyre in Syria to unload its cargo (the precise nautical detail, Robertson); at Tyre, the ancient Phoenician city now having a church, Paul would stay a week with the disciples (v.4), keeping the Lord's Day (Ellicott).

themes: we discovered Cyprus, left it on the left hand
sailed into Syria, and landed at Tyre
the ship to unlade her burden

schools.summary: The Tyre disciples warned Paul 'through the Spirit' not to go — the inspiration of warning, not prohibition: the Spirit revealed the danger (the bonds, 20:23), but the disciples added the dissuasion as their own inference from love (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson); Ellicott: they foresaw the danger but lacked the higher inspiration guiding Paul, who was right to go.

Calvin: diverse gifts of the Spirit — those excelling in prophecy may lack judgment of what is expedient; the Lord showed the brethren what would come, but they knew not Paul's calling, so dissuaded him from love.

debates.question: How can the Spirit forbid Paul to go (v.4) yet Paul go bound by the Spirit (20:22)?

sides.position: The Spirit revealed the danger; the disciples added the dissuasion as their own loving inference, not a divine command — Paul rightly persisted

themes: said to Paul through the Spirit
that he should not go up to Jerusalem

schools.summary: After the week at Tyre, the whole church — with wives and children (the first mention of children, Robertson) — escorted Paul out, and they knelt on the shore and prayed (the beach a place of prayer, Ellicott; an extempore prayer, Barnes); the tender farewell and warm fellowship of the early church.

themes: they all brought us on our way, with wives and children
we kneeled down on the shore, and prayed

schools.summary: After the leave-taking (the kiss of peace, Clarke), Paul embarked and the disciples returned home — a public but affectionate parting on the beach (Robertson); the close of the Tyre visit and resumption of the voyage toward Jerusalem.

themes: we took ship; and they returned home again

schools.summary: The ship reached Ptolemais (the ancient Accho, modern Acre, the best harbor on the coast, Barnes, Robertson), where Paul greeted the brethren and stayed one day — the church there already established (perhaps by the scattered believers, 11:19, or Philip); Paul's diligence even on brief stops.

themes: we came to Ptolemais, and saluted the brethren
Paul's diligence in greeting the brethren

schools.summary: Paul's company came to Caesarea and lodged with Philip the evangelist, one of the seven (6:5) — last seen after the eunuch's baptism (8:40), now risen from deacon to evangelist (Henry); Caesarea where the gospel first reached the Gentiles (Cornelius, ch. 10); Philip's hospitality.

Calvin: the evangelists were between apostles and doctors — a function next to the apostles, preaching the gospel in all places without a fixed station; Philip, once a deacon, raised to this greater charge.

themes: came unto Caesarea, into the house of Philip the evangelist

schools.summary: Philip's four virgin daughters prophesied — women exercising the gift (the fulfillment of Joel, Acts 2:17, Henry, Barnes); Robertson notes the difficulty of reconciling Paul's allowance (1 Corinthians 11:5) with his restriction (14:34); Ellicott sees an order of consecrated 'virgins.'

Calvin: since women bear no public office in the church, they prophesied privately, at home, not in the common assembly — not overthrowing the order God set down; a great gift honoring Philip's house.

debates.question: How did the daughters' prophesying relate to women's public role?

sides.position: They prophesied privately, not in the public assembly — reconciling 1 Corinthians 11:5 with 14:34
The reconciliation is difficult; Paul's allowance and restriction are not easily harmonized

themes: four daughters, virgins, which did prophesy
women exercising the gift of prophecy

schools.summary: During the longer stay at Caesarea, Agabus the prophet (the same who foretold the famine, 11:28, Robertson) came down from Judea — apparently to meet Paul and deliver a prophecy; the divine forewarning of the bonds ahead.
Calvin: the Lord meant to make known Paul's bonds, that the brethren might see he entered the combat willingly and was God's appointed champion — the prophecy for the strengthening of others.

themes: there came down from Judaea a certain prophet, Agabus
the approach of the great prophecy

schools.summary: Agabus took Paul's girdle, bound his own hands and feet, and prophesied the Jews would so bind Paul and deliver him to the Gentiles — a symbolic act in the manner of the OT prophets (Jeremiah, Isaiah, Ezekiel, Barnes, Clarke); precisely fulfilled (v.33), echoing Jesus's own prediction (Matthew 20:19, Robertson).

themes: he took Paul's girdle, and bound his own hands and feet
so shall the Jews at Jerusalem bind the man
the symbolic prophecy of the OT prophets
delivered into the hands of the Gentiles

schools.summary: After Agabus's prophecy, all — including Paul's own companions (Luke admitting he joined, Ellicott) and the Caesarean disciples — begged Paul not to go, fearing for his life; they understood the prophecy as conditional (Clarke), though Paul took it as a command to go (Wesley).
Calvin: their entreaty was worthy of praise — desiring to provide for the church's good by keeping Paul; love prompting the dissuasion, though it could not move him from his calling.

themes: both we, and they of that place, besought him
not to go up to Jerusalem

schools.summary: Paul, deeply moved ('what mean ye to weep and break my heart?' — his intense sensitivity, Ellicott) yet firm, declared his readiness not only to be bound but to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus — the true martyr spirit (Barnes), like Jesus setting his face to Jerusalem (Luke 9:51, Robertson).

themes: what mean ye to weep and to break mine heart
ready not to be bound only, but also to die
for the name of the Lord Jesus

schools.summary: Finding Paul immovable (his will not even bent, Robertson), the disciples ceased and submitted: 'the will of the Lord be done' — yielding to God's will (Barnes), in words echoing the Lord's Prayer and Gethsemane (Matthew 6:10; Luke 22:42, Ellicott).
Calvin: they yield lest they resist the Holy Spirit, by whom they understand Paul is governed; the bridle of all our affections — nothing so bitter that God's will may not mitigate it.

themes: when he would not be persuaded, we ceased
the will of the Lord be done
yielding to the Spirit's leading in Paul

schools.summary: After the days at Caesarea, Paul took up his baggage ('carriages,' the things carried) and went up to Jerusalem (the customary idiom for the holy city) — resolutely setting out despite the warnings, with his company and the collection for the poor saints.

themes: the final stage of the journey

schools.summary: Certain Caesarean disciples accompanied Paul and brought Mnason of Cyprus, an old (long-standing) disciple, with whom the company would lodge — Christian hospitality and a safe lodging among friends; Mnason perhaps an early convert from Cyprus like Barnabas.

themes: certain of the disciples of Caesarea went with us
Mnason of Cyprus, an old disciple
the host and lodging at Jerusalem

schools.summary: When Paul came to Jerusalem, the brethren received him gladly — a warm, sincere welcome (Calvin), distinct from the formal meeting with James the next day (v.18); the fellowship of the church despite the tensions the Judaizers had stirred.

themes: when we were come to Jerusalem

schools.summary: The next day Paul went with his company to James (the Lord's brother, head of the Jerusalem church, as at the Council, ch. 15), and all the elders were present — a formal gathering of the church's leadership, no apostles mentioned, James presiding (Chrysostom).

themes: Paul went in with us unto James
the formal meeting with the leadership

schools.summary: Paul declared in detail what God had wrought among the Gentiles by his ministry — recounting the conversions and churches one by one, ascribing all to God (the work God's, Paul the instrument, Henry, Calvin); the humble, detailed report magnifying God's grace.

themes: he declared particularly what God had wrought among the Gentiles

schools.summary: James and the elders glorified God at the report (rejoicing in the gospel's spread, Barnes), then noted the many thousands of Jewish believers, all zealous for the law — still observing the Mosaic rites (God-appointed, they trained to them, abolition left to providence, Barnes); the Judaizers had prejudiced them against Paul (Robertson).

themes: how many thousands of Jews believe, all zealous of the law

schools.summary: The Judaizers spread the false rumor that Paul taught the dispersion Jews to forsake Moses, circumcision, and the customs — 'apostasy from Moses' (Robertson); a slander (Paul did not preach thus, Clarke), distorting his teaching that the law was not necessary for justification or binding on Gentiles (Barnes); Ellicott notes a grain of truth ('circumcision is nothing') but the personal accusation was false.

themes: thou teachest the Jews to forsake Moses
not to circumcise their children, nor walk after the customs

schools.summary: James asks 'what is to be done?' — knowing the multitude would inevitably gather, hearing Paul had come (Robertson, Barnes); the zealous believers eager to see how he acted, his avoiding them increasing suspicion (Calvin); the need for a plan to allay the suspicion.
Calvin: it would be absurd for so famous an apostle to hide; the elders, seeking to nourish concord and prevent offense, propose a course of action.

themes: the multitude must needs come together

schools.summary: James advised (not commanded, a point of prudence, Gill) that Paul join four men of the Jerusalem church who had a Nazarite vow (Numbers 6) — its completion involving the shaving of the head and sacrifices (Barnes, Clarke); James himself lived as a Nazarite (Ellicott).
Calvin: the elders' counsel, perhaps too indulgent toward the zealous, aimed at concord — tolerating the believers' lingering attachment to the law (the vow 'superstitious') to avoid greater harm.

themes: do this that we say to thee
four men which have a vow on them

schools.summary: Paul was to join the vow, purify himself, and pay for the sacrifices, so all might know the slander false and that he kept the law; Barnes and Clarke defend it as a lawful matter of expediency — showing respect for Moses without compromising the gospel of grace, the law still tolerated.
Calvin: Paul did not dissemble but sincerely professed he did not hate the law — removing the slander that he was an apostate from it, a lawful act to win the weak.
Wesley sharply questions it: Paul ought to have plainly declared the law abolished (as Peter did not keep it, and God commanded him not to); too great a deference to men's judgment.

debates.question: Was Paul's accommodation (joining the vow, keeping the law publicly) lawful condescension or compromising dissimulation?

sides.position: Lawful condescension in things indifferent — to win the weak, showing respect for Moses without denying the gospel (no dissembling)
A flawed deference — Paul should have plainly declared the law abolished

themes: purify thyself with them, be at charges
all may know that thou keepest the law

schools.summary: James reaffirms the apostolic decree (15:20, 29) — the Gentile believers bound to no such thing, only the four abstentions for concord (Robertson); the advice to Paul concerned the Jewish believers, the Gentiles' freedom standing unchanged (Barnes), guarding the gospel of grace.
Calvin: the same liberty belonged to both, the Gentiles named because the Jews clung to their observances; James upholding Gentile freedom while addressing the Jewish believers' zeal.

themes: as touching the Gentiles which believe

schools.summary: Paul took the men, purified himself, and entered the temple to announce the vow's completion — conforming to the advice; largely vindicated: he did no more than at Cenchrea (18:18), violating no truth (Barnes); the act broke the Judaizers' slander over the believers (Robertson).
Calvin: condescension to win the weak, not dissembling — Paul freely submitting to the weak brethren in things indifferent, his conscience not tied.
Wesley questions it: yielding to advice that flowed not from spiritual but carnal wisdom, seeming to be what he was not; Clarke defends it (vows referred to thanksgiving, not justification).

themes: Paul took the men, purifying himself, entered the temple
to signify the accomplishment of the days of purification

schools.summary: As the seven days neared their end, the Asian Jews (Paul's enemies from Ephesus, not Jewish Christians, Robertson) saw him in the temple, stirred up the crowd, and seized him; the sharp irony (Henry) — Paul, conforming to the law to allay suspicion, seized by those falsely accusing him of profaning the temple.

themes: the Jews which were of Asia saw him in the temple
stirred up all the people, and laid hands on him

schools.summary: The Asian Jews cried false charges — that Paul taught against the people, law, and temple, and defiled the temple by bringing in Greeks — resembling those against Stephen (6:13), mixing truth and falsehood (Robertson); a malicious untruth (Clarke), the defiling charge a capital offense to inflame the crowd.
Calvin: the furious hatred — crying as if all religion were in danger, conceiving a false opinion and betraying their venom against the innocent.

themes: against the people, the law, and this place
brought Greeks into the temple, polluted this holy place

schools.summary: The charge rested on a false inference — the Asian Jews had seen Trophimus the Ephesian (20:4) with Paul in the city and supposed he had brought him into the temple (Robertson), though they had not seen him there; Barnes's lesson: slander often rests on inference, not fact; Robertson notes the mob-mind rushing the innocent to death on supposition.

themes: they had seen Trophimus an Ephesian with him in the city
whom they supposed Paul had brought into the temple

schools.summary: The whole city was thrown into tumult (the cry spreading like wildfire, Robertson), and they dragged Paul out of the temple, the doors shut — with the design to kill him (Barnes), the doors shut to prevent sanctuary and to kill him outside without defiling the holy place (Clarke, Wesley).
Calvin: the perverse zeal — the city moved against Paul before hearing his cause, condemning him unheard, the mark of mad rashness, not true zeal.

themes: all the city was moved, the people ran together
drew him out of the temple, the doors were shut

schools.summary: As the mob sought to kill Paul, word reached the chief captain (Claudius Lysias, the chiliarch commanding the cohort in the Tower of Antonia overlooking the temple, Barnes, Ellicott) that all Jerusalem was in uproar; the Roman guard on alert during the feast.
Calvin: the providence — God raising up the captain suddenly to deliver Paul from death without man's counsel, the Roman coming to quell the riot and saving the apostle.

themes: as they went about to kill him
tidings came unto the chief captain
God's providence raising up the captain

schools.summary: The chief captain swiftly took soldiers and centurions (a large force, Robertson) and ran down from the tower, and the mob stopped beating Paul at the sight of them — overawed by the Roman presence, before the job was over (Robertson).
Calvin: those unrestrained by God's majesty or the temple relent before a soldier — showing they acted from barbarous cruelty, not zeal; God's deliverance through the Roman power.

themes: took soldiers and centurions, and ran down

schools.summary: The chief captain took Paul and commanded him bound with two chains (fulfilling Agabus's prophecy, v.11) — bound between two soldiers (Barnes, Clarke, as 12:6) as a dangerous prisoner — and demanded who he was and what he had done; Lysias repeating his inquiries (Robertson).

themes: commanded him to be bound with two chains
demanded who he was, and what he had done

schools.summary: The mob shouted contradictory things (as at Ephesus, 19:32, Ellicott), and the captain, unable to learn the truth amid the tumult (Robertson), ordered Paul carried into the castle (the Tower of Antonia).
Calvin: the madness of the mob — contradictory cries, yet all desiring Paul's death though convicted of nothing, devilish madness not true zeal.

themes: some cried one thing, some another
could not know the certainty for the tumult

schools.summary: On the stairs leading to the castle, the violence of the crowd was so great that the soldiers had to carry Paul bodily (literally lifting him, the troops lining the staircase, Ellicott); the perilous removal amid the mob's fury, the Roman guard preserving him.

themes: when he came upon the stairs
borne of the soldiers for the violence of the people

schools.summary: The mob followed, crying 'away with him' — the demand for death (the phrase always meaning death in a Jewish mob, Barnes, Clarke), the very cry raised against Jesus at the crucifixion (Luke 23:18; John 19:15, Ellicott); Paul sharing the sufferings of Christ, the same cry pursuing him (Robertson).

themes: the multitude followed after, crying, Away with him

schools.summary: As Paul was led into the castle, he calmly and courteously asked the chief captain 'May I speak?' in Greek — his amazing self-control amid the mob (Robertson) — and the captain, surprised to hear Greek (expecting Hebrew, Ellicott), asked if he could speak it.
Calvin: Paul's purpose to defend his cause and make his integrity known, as all God's servants must, lest God's name be blasphemed through their infamy.

themes: the request to defend his cause

schools.summary: The captain mistook Paul for the Egyptian false prophet who, under Felix, led a multitude to the Mount of Olives and escaped (Josephus); on the number discrepancy (Luke's 4,000 vs Josephus's 30,000), the witnesses defend Luke — Josephus exaggerating, or Luke referring to the armed core (Sicarii), or a copyist confusing Δ (four) with Λ (thirty), or different stages (Barnes, Clarke).

debates.question: How to reconcile Luke's 4,000 with Josephus's 30,000 for the Egyptian's followers?

sides.position: Defend Luke — Josephus exaggerated, or Luke counts the armed core, or a copyist's error (Δ/Λ), or different stages; Luke not less reliable than Josephus

themes: four thousand men that were murderers

schools.summary: Paul corrected the captain — 'a Jew of Tarsus... a citizen of no mean city' — declaring his nationality and standing as a citizen of the renowned Tarsus (rival to Athens and Alexandria, Barnes, Robertson), and boldly requested to address the people; a strange, daring request to the murderous mob (Robertson).
Chrysostom: Paul declares his religion ('I am a Jew') — being both a Jew and a Christian, observing what he ought; drawing the captain off the surmise of the Egyptian.

themes: I am a Jew of Tarsus, a citizen of no mean city
suffer me to speak unto the people
the bold request to address the mob

schools.summary: The captain gave leave (satisfied Paul was no rebel, Calvin), and Paul, standing on the stairs, beckoned for silence (as 12:17) and addressed the people in Hebrew (Aramaic) — the language conciliating them (Barnes), subduing them by its familiarity (Chrysostom: they had an awe for it).

themes: he gave him licence, Paul stood on the stairs
beckoned with the hand, spake in the Hebrew tongue
